quotes.quote: Nothing I do seems to fix this slice I'm dealing with.
I would do anything to hit driver like that
My buddy Sterling hits a big ol nasty slice. We were playing in a scramble and my buddy Matt piped a baby draw down the fairway as he's known to do.
I don't know anyone that has fixed a slice on their own; I tried... didn't work.
Your issue is either in your setup, backswing, or transition to downswing... or maybe a problem in all three.
Trying to fix more than one issue at a time is a recipe for bad golf and frustration.
I had to literally break my swing/stance/grip/alignment down to basics to fix it. Now I fixed it, but it was frustrating.
Most of what I fixed was my grip (lead hand rotated to see three knuckles) and shallowing my swing.
Almost 1200rpm side spin... Isn't really ok. Face or path issue or both.
As a veteran slicer, my guess is the combination of (a) an out to in swing and (b) the club face is a bit open compared to the swing path. Both of which send the ball to the right.
A quick fix that helps me when I am leaving the face open is to either strengthen my grip or go ahead and just rotate the club face closed at address.
True, but if you get into that kind of expenditure and you're still hitting consistent bananas, you need a new teacher.
I'm hitting a lot fewer bananas but the push is still an issue, meaning club face open but the swing path is now out to in.

I believe just like most others have said you are having a out to in path along with a open club face but its just inconsistent. I fought this problem and still do at times as I feel it never leaves haha.
You already know there's only ONE reason you slice: an open clubface relative to the path of your swing.
If you come over the top it's robbing you of accuracy, distance, and consistency.
An outside-in swing path makes you cut across the ball and hit the 'outside' of the golf ball. That adds the clockwise spin that sends the ball off to the right.
Golfers who hit 'down' at the ball are wasting almost all their power by sending it into the ground... And losing at least 17-40+ yards of distance.

See, you can try ANY 'swing fix' like flipping your wrists, strengthening your grip, or trying to 'keep the clubface square'... But if your face is open to the path... or if you are hitting down at the ball... You'll never stop slicing OR reach your maximum potential off the tee.
You can be perfectly on-plane and use every trick in the book to build Ben Hogan-like lag into your swing but... If You Don't Square The Face With A Positive Attack Angle - You'll Never Hit A Draw

You can even hit the gym and add 5-10 mph of swing speed... But if you hit down on the ball... You'll never reach your maximum distance potential because most of your energy will be going into the ground instead of the air.
99 out of 100 Amateurs Don't Have The Time (Or Patience) To Do This On Their Own
Because that requires expensive lessons... MORE TIME on the range... you DON'T HAVE to get results... Plus a LOT of patience. And it makes it nearly impossible to enjoy the game.
I've fixed more amateur slices than anyone on the planet. I've given over 70,000 in-person lessons to struggling amateurs...
I had made so many little adjustments through the years trying to fix my slice
I can fix my slice but I prefer to use my real swing.
Slices are one of the most frustrating mistakes in golf, sending your ball into the rough, trees, or even out of bounds, making low scores almost impossible.
Plus, slices rob you of distance, leaving you with longer approach shots.
If you've been practicing constantly but still struggle with slicing, you might need a more structured approach and the right training tools.
A slice happens when the clubface is open at impact relative to the swing path. While that might seem simple, there are underlying factors that cause the clubface to open in the first place.
A weak grip can lead to an open clubface at impact, making it difficult to control the ball's flight.
Many slices are caused by an over-the-top swing path, which cuts across the ball from outside to inside, leaving the clubface open.
If your body isn't rotating properly during the swing, your arms tend to take over, causing an open clubface and a slice.
A grip that's too weak (hands rotated too far under the club) can make it difficult to square the face at impact.
A major cause of slicing is an outside-to-inside swing path. To fix this, you need to train your body to swing more from the inside.

A slice - the ball starting left (for right-handed players) and then curving hard right - is one of the most frustrating shots in golf. It robs you of distance, wastes strokes, and leaves you playing from the rough or even out of bounds.
The good news is that a slice isn't a mystery; it's the result of a few fixable swing faults.
If your hands are positioned incorrectly on the club, the clubface stays open at impact and the ball spins to the right.
An open stance or misaligned shoulders directs your swing path from outside to inside, adding sidespin.
Over-the-top/out-to-in swing path: This happens when the upper body dominates the downswing, causing the club to cut across the ball.
A cupped left wrist or weak grip keeps the face open during impact, leading to slices.
Keeping your weight on your back foot reduces your ability to square the clubface.
Without a video of your swing it's difficult to know why you in particular are slicing. But there are 2 general reasons for a slice.
An outside the line to inside the line swing path. This means as your club approaches the ball, it approaches from a point that is outside the target line.
Because the swing crosses the line outside to inside, you will impart a clockwise spin on the ball which will lead to the ball turning to the right (for the right handed golfer).
Regardless of all things, you may have a swing path that is beautiful, but your clubface is open at impact. That will also produce a slice.

Contrary to what most golfers do, the fix is complex. The golfers' tendency is to open up more and pull harder left to get the ball moving left. What this normally does is impart MORE spin so the ball will go harder right.
It's hard to do because it is counter intuitive - my shot is going right, why am I aiming right? But turning the ball over is the way to defeat the slice.
An OPEN face relative to the PATH is the ONE and ONLY real REASON you hit a slice

Most players KNOW that. And while I could break down the old and new ball-flight laws that prove it... The bottom line is... All slice-fix training, draw-biased clubs, and virtually every training aid to fix a slice... All 'attempt' to do the SAME THING for you... Square your clubface relative to the path at impact.
If the clubface is open even 1 degree to the path of the club... your ball will slice. If it's open 2 or more degrees... Well, that's when you're losing balls in the water or searching in the woods.
Most slicers think they can aim left to compensate for a slice. Truth is... aiming left just creates a bigger gap in the face-to-path number... And results in an even larger pull slice...
You don't have the time to practice long enough, OR consistently enough to fix your slice the HARD WAY.

Whether it's changing your grip... Or working on your release... Or even swinging from the inside... That practice time ALL takes away from the reason we golf in the first place... to PLAY - and have fun.
Slicing is one of the most common problems that golfers face, whether they are beginners or seasoned players. A slice can ruin your game, leading to frustration and lost strokes.
Understanding how to stop slicing in golf is crucial for improving your consistency and overall performance on the course.

This phenomenon often occurs when the clubface is open at impact, causing the ball to spin in a way that makes it veer off course. Slices can result in lost distance and accuracy, making it essential to address this issue.
An improper grip can lead to an open clubface at impact.
Misaligned feet and shoulders can cause the club to swing along the wrong path.
Poor swing path or technique can exacerbate slicing.
Using the wrong golf clubs or balls can also affect your swing and increase the likelihood of slicing.
If you've tried self-correcting your slice without success, it may be time to seek professional help.
I see FAR too many slicers with an extreme over-the-top swing... And I see those same players out on the range banging balls aimlessly. I don't want that for you.
It reverses your over-the-top move - in just 5 minutes of practice swings each week... Giving you a draw-producing path.

If you swing 'through the gates and over the hurdles' you'll know you are swinging inside-out AND up on the ball... which is the magic combination you need to draw the ball off the tee and hit it FAR.
If you hit the Swing Gates you'll know your path is outside in, setting you up to put that 'slice spin' on the golf ball and hit those frustrating shots to the right
If you clip the Launch Hurdles you'll know you are swinging DOWN on the ball... robbing yourself of 40+ yards of distance

Stop searching for swing-fix solutions that don't work... Stop hoping that heel-weighted or offset drivers will counteract your slice... Stop waiting for big manufacturers to make a club to fix your slice... Stop trying to find more time to re-train your swing to fix your clubface at impact
It works no matter your age... No matter your swing speed... And no matter how long you've been slicing.
permanently eliminate the slice that's stealing the fun from your game
